linguisticChallenges.bokmålVsNynorsk: Norway has two official written standards: Bokmål (based on Dano-Norwegian) and Nynorsk (based on rural dialects). MT must target the correct one — they are different enough that mixing them is immediately noticeable. 'nb' targets Bokmål.
linguisticChallenges.threeGenders: Norwegian Bokmål can use either two genders (common/neuter like Danish) or three genders (masculine/feminine/neuter). The three-gender system is more colloquial and increasingly common. MT should be consistent about which system it uses.
linguisticChallenges.compoundNouns: Like other Scandinavian languages, Norwegian forms compounds by concatenation with linking elements.
linguisticChallenges.scandinavianMixing: MT models have particular difficulty distinguishing Norwegian from Danish due to their extreme similarity in written form. Vocabulary differences (e.g., 'snakke' NO vs 'tale' DA for 'speak') must be correct.

dialects.classification: Bokmål and Nynorsk are both official written standards. Bokmål is used by ~85-90% of the population. Spoken dialects vary enormously and have high social prestige.

encyclopedic.history: Bokmål evolved from Dano-Norwegian, the written language used during Norway's union with Denmark (1380–1814). Nynorsk was created by Ivar Aasen in the 19th century as a 'purer' Norwegian based on rural dialects.
